Small-area estimation of childhood stunting, anaemia, infant-feeding inadequacy and diarrhoea across the 261 districts of Ghana

A Bayesian spatial and machine-learning analysis of the 2022 Demographic and Health Survey
Valentine Golden Ghanem  ·  Ghana COCOBOD Cocoa Clinic, Accra, Ghana  ·  ORCID 0009-0002-8332-0220  ·  valentineghanem@gmail.com

Background

Across sub-Saharan Africa an estimated 35% of children aged 6–59 months are stunted and 64% are anaemic. Ghana's national stunting fell from 33% in 1993 to 17% in 2022 — but the national mean hides a near three-fold regional gradient, from 10% in Eastern Region to 30% in the north.

Ghana plans and budgets child-nutrition programmes at the district level, yet the Demographic and Health Survey is powered only to the 16 regions. District managers therefore have no directly-measured estimates to act on. No previous study has produced joint, multi-outcome small-area estimates for Ghana's 261 districts.

Methods

Design. Ecological, cross-sectional, spatially-resolved analysis of all 261 metropolitan, municipal and district assemblies (MMDAs); reference year 2022.

  • Outcomes — 2022 GDHS region direct estimates (16 regions = effective inferential unit): stunting, anaemia (6–59 m), infant and young child feeding (IYCF) inadequacy, diarrhoea.
  • Covariates — 2021 Population & Housing Census, at district level (poverty, illiteracy, NHIS cover, under-15 share, urbanicity, water and sanitation).
  • Small-area estimation — benchmarked Besag–York– Mollié (BYM2) Bayesian model over a queen-contiguity graph of the 261 districts; district means benchmarked to region direct estimates.
  • Spatial analysis — Global Moran's I, Local Indicators of Spatial Association (LISA), bivariate LISA, Getis-Ord Gi*.
  • Non-stationarity — geographically weighted regression (GWR) with an adaptive bi-square kernel.
  • Determinants — Random Forest and XGBoost interpreted with SHAP; spatial leave-one-region-out cross-validation (16 folds).
  • Reporting — STROBE, RECORD-Spatial, TRIPOD+AI.

Results

17
districts in the quadruple-burden core — hotspots for all four outcomes at once
0.82–0.96
Global Moran's I across the four surfaces (all p = 0.002)
0.78
stunting × anaemia bivariate Moran's I; 49 joint High-High districts
225–368
GWR ΔAICc over OLS — effects are spatially non-stationary

Modelled district posterior prevalence spanned 7.5–34.6% (stunting), 30.5–74.4% (anaemia), 58.5–91.8% (IYCF inadequacy) and 4.2–34.1% (diarrhoea). All four surfaces were strongly spatially clustered and co-located.

Determinants

SHAP analysis identified water and sanitation access, female illiteracy and poverty as the dominant determinants of the district burden surface. Improved water and poverty incidence did not enter the small-area model, so their importance is independent of it.

The four outcomes co-located most strongly for stunting and anaemia (bivariate Moran's I = 0.78; 49 joint High-High districts).

Quadruple-burden core

Hotspot districts — all four outcomes

Posterior exceedance probability > 0.95 for all four outcomes (17 districts):
Northern (14): Gushegu Municipal, Karaga, Kpandai, Kumbungu, Mion, Nanton, Nanumba North Municipal, Nanumba South, Saboba, Savelugu Municipal, Tatale Sanguli, Tolon, Yendi Municipal, Zabzugu.
Savannah (3): Central Gonja, North East Gonja, North Gonja.

Conclusions

Childhood stunting, anaemia, infant-feeding inadequacy and diarrhoea in Ghana are not four separate problems but a single, spatially-concentrated syndemic. The modelled district burden surface converges on a compact 17-district core in Northern and Savannah Regions. Determinant effects vary geographically, so a single national model is inadequate. District estimates are model-based posteriors, read with their credible intervals.

Policy implications

  • A national-mean framing cannot allocate resources — a defined 17-district core can.
  • Concentrate integrated water, sanitation, maternal-education and nutrition investment on the quadruple-burden core.
  • Calibrate intervention design regionally rather than as a uniform national package.
  • The reproducible small-area-estimation pipeline is a transferable tool for district-targeted, equity-focused policy.

Key references

  1. Takele BA, Gezie LD, Alamneh TS. Pooled prevalence of stunting and associated factors among children aged 6-59 months in Sub-Saharan Africa: a Bayesian multilevel approach. PLoS One. 2022;17(10):e0275889.
  2. Tesema GA, Tessema ZT, Tamirat KS, Teshale AB. Prevalence and determinants of severity levels of anemia among children aged 6-59 months in sub-Saharan Africa. PLoS One. 2021;16(4):e0249978.
  3. Ghana Statistical Service, Ghana Health Service, ICF. Ghana Demographic and Health Survey 2022. Accra and Rockville (MD): GSS and ICF; 2024. Report FR387.
  4. Aheto JMK, Dagne GA. Geostatistical analysis, web-based mapping and environmental determinants of under-5 stunting: 2014 Ghana DHS. Lancet Planet Health. 2021;5(6):e347-e355.
  5. Mercer LD, Wakefield J, Pantazis A, et al. Space-time smoothing of complex survey data: small area estimation for child mortality. Ann Appl Stat. 2015;9(4):1889-1905.
  6. Riebler A, Sorbye SH, Simpson D, Rue H. An intuitive Bayesian spatial model for disease mapping that accounts for scaling. Stat Methods Med Res. 2016;25(4):1145-1165.

Data & reporting

Data: Ghana DHS 2022 (GSS, GHS, ICF); 2021 Population & Housing Census (GSS).
Reporting: STROBE · RECORD-Spatial · TRIPOD+AI.
Ethics: public aggregate data; no new approval. Funding: none. Competing interests: none.
